metadata.summary: Design a token-efficient LLM classifier: pick model by task, force structured output via tool_use, strip auto-loaded SDK context, batch inputs, and amortise cache spend over the run.

content.title: LLM Classifier Design (tool-use, minimum context, batched)
content.summary: An LLM classifier should answer one question per item with the smallest possible payload. The right design decisions — pick the model that just barely meets the bar, force the answer through a single tool call, replace the SDK's default system prompt with your own, disable auto-loaded project context, batch items by numeric ID, and accept that an SDK turn includes the user message — collapse a multi-step agent loop into a single forced tool_use response and let prompt caching amortise the system prefix across the run.

why.p: "Just ask the model and parse the JSON" is the path that burns ten times more output tokens than necessary, hits rate limits inside a single 5-hour window, and produces brittle parsers that misread thinking blocks as answers. A classifier is a structured-output problem, not a chat problem: the model should emit one tool call with one boolean (or one enum) per item and nothing else. Every other token is overhead — the model's, the SDK's, the project context the wrapper auto-loads, the per-call system prefix that the cache could have re-used. Minimising each of those layers in turn is what separates a $5 run from a $50 run on the same task.

list.item: Yes/no or small-enum classification across many items (sufficiency audit, spam triage, intent detection, content gating, deduplication).
Document scoring where the score is a fixed-shape object (rubric grading, relevance ranking, safety verdict).
Bulk extraction with a known schema (named entities, citations, contact details).
Any pipeline where the model's job is "look at this and produce one structured answer," not "have a conversation."
Open-ended generation where the answer shape is unknown — use plain text completion.
Multi-step reasoning where the model genuinely benefits from intermediate scratchpad turns — use Extended Thinking, not a forced single tool call.
Tiny one-shot requests where building a tool schema and an SDK config costs more developer time than the cost saved.

section.name: 1. Frame the task before picking the model
section.p: Write the question the classifier answers in one sentence. Decide the answer's exact shape: a boolean, a small enum (≤10 values), a fixed-keys object, an array of those. Write the *failure mode* you care about — false positives or false negatives — and pick the model that clears that bar with margin, not the cheapest one that *might* clear it.

list.item: Pure binary on short, structured input (≤2K tokens, clear signal): Haiku is the floor; verify with a 50-item gold set.
Binary on noisy/long input, or small enum: Sonnet — still cheap, still fast, handles ambiguity.
Subtle judgement, multi-criterion, or anything where you'd ask a senior engineer: Opus — pay the premium once, don't rerun.
Inputs near or above 200K tokens per item: switch to the 1M-context variant of the chosen model (Sonnet has a `[1m]` form on paid extra-usage; Opus has 1M as standard).

section.p: Build a 30–100-item evaluation set with hand-labelled answers before the first batch. Lock the model only after it scores acceptably. Re-test whenever you change the prompt, the schema, or the model.

section.name: 2. Force the answer through a single tool call
section.p: Define exactly one tool whose schema is the answer shape. Set `tool_choice` to that tool by name (Anthropic SDK) or list it as the only `allowed_tools` entry (Claude Agent SDK). The model can no longer "explain its reasoning" in prose — it can only call the tool. Output token cost collapses to roughly the JSON of the answer.
Never parse the model's free text. If you see yourself running `re.search` or `json.loads` on assistant prose, the design is wrong — go back and add the tool.

section.name: 3. Strip every byte the SDK adds for free
section.p: Wrappers like Claude Agent SDK and Cursor/Continue load a system prompt of their own (≈10–15K tokens of built-in tool descriptions, behaviours, hints) plus any `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` files they discover walking up from the working directory (another 3–10K tokens). For a classifier none of that helps — the model only needs the criterion and the schema.

list.item: Replace the preset system prompt by passing your own string. Do not append; replace.
Disable project-context auto-load via `setting_sources=[]` (Claude Agent SDK) or run from a directory that has no `CLAUDE.md`/`AGENTS.md` chain.
Restrict tools to your one tool via `allowed_tools=["mcp__yours__verdict"]`. The model still sees other tools in the cached prefix but cannot call them.

Block the deferred-tool resolver: Claude Code lazily loads MCP tool schemas via a `ToolSearch` call, which costs a turn. Add `disallowed_tools=["ToolSearch"]` so the schema lands in the initial context and the model goes straight to your tool.
Run with `bypassPermissions` to avoid the permission-prompt round-trip.

section.p: Counter-intuitive trap: stripping all built-in tool descriptions (e.g. `--tools ""`) often makes things worse — with no tools visible, sonnet falls back to Extended Thinking and spends 10× the output tokens reasoning before the tool call. Leave the built-ins alone; just don't allow them.

section.name: 4. Accept the SDK's 2-turn floor
section.p: Claude Agent SDK counts every message — user and assistant — as a turn. A single forced tool-use response is therefore `num_turns=2` (one user, one assistant). Setting `max_turns=1` produces an `error_max_turns` exception even though the tool was successfully called, because the SDK accounts for the user message you just sent. The minimum is `max_turns=2`. Anything you read in docs about "1 turn = 1 model action" is the model's perspective; the SDK's accounting is different. Do not waste effort trying to drop below 2.

section.name: 5. Batch items by numeric ID
section.p: Per-item calls pay the system-prefix overhead (cached but not free) on every call. Pack N items into one prompt and ask the tool for an array of N answers keyed by their position. This converts O(N) calls into O(N/B) calls.

Empirical cap: Sonnet truncates the `results` array around 15–20 entries, regardless of `max_tokens`. Increasing the limit doesn't help — the model just stops emitting items. Default to `batch_size=10` for safety, push to 15 only if you measure the model still emits the full array. Do not chase batch_size=30 or 100: you will lose half the answers and waste the call.
Always include a per-item ID in the prompt and require it back in the answer. Position-only matching (zip on order) silently corrupts when the model drops one item from the middle.

section.name: 6. Amortise the system prefix with prompt caching
section.p: The system prompt + tool schema + custom instructions are byte-identical across every batch — that's exactly what prompt caching is for. With caching enabled (default for Anthropic API and Claude Agent SDK), the first call writes the prefix to cache (cache_w; ≈1.25× input cost), and every subsequent call within the cache window reads it (cache_r; ≈0.1× input cost).

list.item: First batch is expensive — accept that one cache write happens. After that, per-batch cost drops 5–10×.
Keep the system prefix byte-identical between batches. A single trailing newline change invalidates the cache. Centralise the prompt in a constant; do not interpolate per-batch values into it.
Put per-batch variability after the cached prefix: cached system + tool schema first, then the variable user message with the documents.
Don't restart the process per batch if you can avoid it. Some SDKs spawn a fresh subprocess per `query()` call and lose the in-process cache key — measure your stack.

example.code: # Cache-busting: per-batch timestamp in the cached prefix SYSTEM = f"You are an auditor. Run started {datetime.now()}. ..." # different every batch
SYSTEM = "You are an auditor. Reply only via the verdicts tool." # identical bytes # variability lives in the user message

section.name: 7. Parallelism vs rate limits
section.p: For most subscription-billed setups, more than ~3 concurrent classifier sessions trips per-minute or per-account caps and produces silent `exit code 1` failures from the CLI sub-process. Sequential is the safe default; small parallelism (2–3) is the sweet spot.

list.item: Always retry the failed shard. Rate-limit failures cluster; the survivors finish faster than the failures retry.
Use shell parallelism (xargs -P, GNU parallel) rather than asyncio inside the classifier itself — it keeps the script sync, easier to debug, and naturally caps at process count.
Each parallel shard primes its own cache if the SDK uses per-process keys. 5 cold caches = 5× cache_w cost. Shard count is a knob; raise it only after measuring.

Exit-1 from the CLI is opaque: enable the `stderr` callback (Claude Agent SDK) or capture sub-process stderr (Anthropic CLI) so you see the real reason — often `429 rate_limited` or `error_max_turns`.

section.name: 8. Track quota and cost per batch
section.p: Cost reported by the SDK (`ResultMessage.total_cost_usd`) is authoritative. Quota burn (5-hour window, 7-day window) lives in a runtime state file the wrapper writes — read it before and after each batch and log the delta.

example.code:
# Per-batch metrics line elapsed_s = round(time.monotonic() - t0, 1) usage = result_msg.usage # input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens cost = result_msg.total_cost_usd
print( f"batch {bi}/{n}: elapsed={elapsed_s}s " f"in={usage['input_tokens']} out={usage['output_tokens']} " f"cache_w={usage['cache_creation_input_tokens']} " f"cache_r={usage['cache_read_input_tokens']} " f"cost=${cost:.4f}" )

section.p: Three signals to watch on every batch: cache_w trending to zero (cache is warm), output_tokens flat with batch size (model is on-task), elapsed jumps (the model fell back to thinking — your prompt or the tool list changed). Any spike in any of those tells you to stop and inspect before you burn the rest of the quota.

section.name: 9. Build the failed-batch retry path before the first run
section.p: You will have failures. Plan for them up-front. Two failure modes dominate:

list.item: Truncation: model returned `n` answers for a `B`-item batch, `n < B`. Detect by ID-matching the response. The missing IDs are the retry list.
Hard error: the call raised. Log every input ID in the batch as failed.

section.p: Persist a per-batch JSON with `audited`, `errors`, `insufficient_per_domain` (or your equivalent), `totals`. Merge across shards. The retry pass takes only the failed IDs and re-runs them with `batch_size=1..3` — small enough that truncation can't happen — usually under a single-thread loop because the survivor count is small.

list.item: Question is one sentence; answer shape is a fixed schema.
Eval set of 30–100 hand-labelled items exists and the model passes it.
Tool defined; `tool_choice` (or `allowed_tools`) forces it; nothing else is in scope.
System prompt is custom, not appended to a preset; no `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` auto-loaded.
`disallowed_tools=["ToolSearch"]`; `permission_mode="bypassPermissions"`.
`max_turns=2` (the SDK floor; lower errors out).
Batch size starts at 10; raise only after measuring full-array returns.
System prefix is byte-identical across batches; no per-batch interpolation in the cached region.
Per-batch metrics line logs elapsed, in/out tokens, cache_w/r, cost.
Retry path takes a `--paths-file` (or equivalent) and runs the survivors at `batch_size<=3`.
Quota state read before and after the run from the runtime state file (not by parsing statusline output).
If a parallel shard hits `exit code 1` cascade, sequential retry is cheaper than tuning concurrency.
